remission.spontaneous_remission_rate: 26% of childhood-onset asthma in clinical remission by adulthood; <20% adult-onset achieves sustained remission; 65% long-term remission in mild-moderate vs 16% in severe
remission.best_intervention_remission_rate: Biologics (anti-IL-5, anti-IL-4/13, anti-IgE) achieve near-zero exacerbation rates in eligible severe patients; allergen immunotherapy (AIT) achieves sustained drug-free remission in allergic asthma in ~30-40% of treated patients
remission.gap_size: Moderate; highly severity-dependent; large practice gap for AIT
remission.barrier_detail: Allergen immunotherapy is the only disease-modifying treatment for allergic asthma and is underused relative to guidelines; biological phenotyping (T2-high vs T2-low) drives outcomes more than treatment intensity alone
remission.last_soc_change: 2022 — GINA guidelines introduced 'anti-inflammatory reliever' strategy (ICS-formoterol as reliever replacing SABA monotherapy); biologics formally endorsed as step 5 since ~2016; ICS as first-line unchanged since 1990s
remission.outdated_treatments: Short-acting beta-agonist (SABA) monotherapy as reliever without ICS (GINA 2019 explicitly removed SABA-only from recommended options after evidence of harm); oral corticosteroid 'burst' as go-to for exacerbations without underlying biologic assessment
remission.adjunctive_emerging: Bronchial thermoplasty (FDA approved, underutilized in severe non-biologic-eligible patients); vitamin D supplementation (meta-analysis: reduces exacerbation risk 50% in deficient patients); dietary intervention (Mediterranean diet reduces exacerbation frequency)
remission.frontier_signal: Tezepelumab (anti-TSLP) for unselected severe asthma regardless of eosinophil count; dupilumab extended to children 6+ months; CRISPR approaches to IL-4 receptor editing in murine models
remission.notes: Manually curated from landmark trials, registries, and guidelines (disease_db_100.json). Protected from automated overwrite.
